ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for fried tofu dish; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared Southeast Asian soy dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Tahu Goreng is approximately 200 grams, consisting of several pieces of deep-fried tofu topped with crushed peanuts, bean sprouts, cucumber slices, and sweet soy-tamarind sauce.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — Tahu Goreng / Fried Tofu
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Tahu Goreng is listed in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as a Malay/Indonesian soy-based dish. Values for core macros and secondary nutrients are drawn from the HPB record. Micronutrient values (potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, phosphorus, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, vitamins) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried tofu (NDB No. 16301) where HPB does not report the specific micronutrient. Vitamin D and Vitamin B12 are confirmed zero for this plant-based tofu dish.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Soy-based foods and tofu analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Tahu Goreng as a composite dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is estimated based on the analogous entry for fried tofu and soy-based dishes (GI range 15–50 for tofu; sauce components with sweet soy sauce raise composite GI modestly). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (45 × 21.0g) / 100 ≈ 9.5. This is classified as low GL. Estimation is consistent with published values for similar Southeast Asian tofu dishes in Diabetes Care (2008) and the University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (350 mg per 100g; ~700 mg per standard 200g serving) from sweet soy sauce (kicap manis) and added seasonings
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Tahu Goreng due to its high sodium content (~700 mg per serving), which represents approximately 30% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg; requesting reduced-sauce or sauce-on-side preparation can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI ~45) moderated by high protein and dietary fibre, but sugar content from kicap manis (sweet soy sauce) warrants monitoring
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tahu Goreng has a low GI (~45) and a low glycemic load (~9.5 per serving), making it a relatively suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the 4.5g sugar per 100g (9g per serving) from sweet soy sauce should be accounted for in daily carbohydrate budgets, and portion size should be controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying adds total fat (14.8g/100g) with a favorable unsaturated-to-saturated ratio; peanuts contribute beneficial unsaturated fats, but high sodium and deep-frying method are concerns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tahu Goreng provides a favorable fat profile (predominantly polyunsaturated and monounsaturated fats from tofu and peanuts, with low saturated fat at ~2.1g/100g) and soy isoflavones with cardioprotective potential; however, the high sodium content and deep-frying method are cardiovascular risk factors, and individuals with CVD should consume this dish in moderation and prefer preparations with reduced sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Soy isoflavones and peanut polyphenols may offer anti-inflammatory benefits, but deep-frying can promote pro-inflammatory oxidized lipids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Soy protein and peanuts contain compounds with potential anti-inflammatory benefits relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; however, deep-fried foods may promote pro-inflammatory responses in some individuals, and consumption should be moderated.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tahu Goreng is a popular Malay and Indonesian deep-fried tofu dish widely consumed across Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia as a side dish, snack, or street food. It is prepared by deep-frying firm tofu blocks until the exterior is golden and crispy while the interior remains soft and silken. The fried tofu is served with a rich, dark sauce made from sweet soy sauce (kicap manis), tamarind, chili, and garlic, and is generously topped with toasted crushed peanuts, fresh bean sprouts, and sliced cucumber. The dish delivers a harmonious contrast of textures — the crunch of the peanut-coated fried tofu skin against the tender tofu interior and crisp fresh vegetables — and a complex flavor profile ranging from savory and sweet to spicy and tangy. Nutritionally, Tahu Goreng provides approximately 220 kcal per 100g, with 12.5g protein, 14.8g fat (predominantly unsaturated), and 10.5g carbohydrate. It is a meaningful source of calcium (180 mg/100g), iron (2.5 mg/100g), and plant-based protein from soy. The dish has a low glycemic index (~45) due to the high protein and fibre content of tofu and peanuts, making it relatively suitable for individuals managing blood sugar. However, the sodium content (~350 mg/100g) from soy sauce is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Tahu Goreng is vegetarian, vegan, halal, and dairy-free, but contains soy and peanut allergens. It is a culturally significant dish in Southeast Asian cuisine, commonly found at hawker centres, street food stalls, and home kitchens throughout the region.

confidence_reason: Tahu Goreng is listed in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as a canonical Malay/Indonesian soy-based dish. Core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from HPB with supplementary micronutrient data from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for fried tofu. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for soy-based and tofu dishes. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite Tahu Goreng dish (including sauce, peanuts, and vegetables) is not directly reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried tofu entry as a composite dish value; individual component moisture data exists but composite moisture is not available. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): Tahu Goreng is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally absent in plant foods and confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 16301) and HPB records. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): tofu and plant-based ingredients contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): all ingredients are plant-based; cholesterol is naturally zero in plant foods. trans_fat_g is 0.0: no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation; confirmed negligible per HPB and USDA sources. Saturated fat (2.1g), monounsaturated fat (3.2g), and polyunsaturated fat (8.1g) values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried tofu (NDB 16301) and peanut composition data, as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions for this dish. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for tofu and soy-based dishes; no direct GI measurement for composite Tahu Goreng exists in the approved GI database. Glycemic load per serving (200g) = (45 × 21.0g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 9.5 (low GL). Ontology: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are defensible matches for fried tofu food product; SNOMED CT has no specific code for this Southeast Asian prepared dish.
